output:
# From One Threshold to a Risk-Conditional Field: Per-Prompt Safety Gating for Llama 2-Style RLHF (GAP = MONOGATE, METHOD = AEGIS)

A new research idea generated from the Llama 2 Introduction (Touvron et al. 2023, arXiv:2307.09288 [1]). The proposal targets one specific, signature mechanism of the Llama 2-Chat alignment recipe -- the way two reward models are combined during RLHF through a single global safety-gating threshold -- and asks whether that scalar can be promoted to an observable, per-prompt, risk-conditional map that strictly improves the helpfulness-safety trade-off while provably reducing to the original recipe at its default setting.

This document has four sections:
1. Background and current state of the art
2. Key findings and analysis
3. Novel research idea (the five-question / "5q" proposal)
4. References and evidence


## Section 1. Background and Current State of the Art

### 1.1 The Llama 2 alignment recipe and the helpfulness-safety tension

Large Language Models are pretrained as auto-regressive transformers on large self-supervised corpora -- the Llama line [2] among them -- and then aligned to human preferences, most prominently through Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) [3,4,5]. The Llama 2 Introduction [1] frames the central contribution of Llama 2-Chat as a careful, reproducible fine-tuning and safety methodology: supervised fine-tuning (SFT), preference data collection, reward modeling, and iterative RLHF with rejection sampling and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) [6], augmented with safety-specific data annotation, red-teaming, and iterative safety evaluation. A recurring theme in the Introduction and in the body of the paper is that helpfulness and safety can pull in opposite directions: a model tuned only for helpfulness may comply with harmful requests, while a model tuned only for safety may refuse benign ones. This tension is the well-documented "helpful and harmless" trade-off studied since the earliest open RLHF assistants [7].

Llama 2-Chat's distinctive engineering answer to this tension is to train two separate reward models -- a Helpfulness reward model R_h and a Safety reward model R_s -- and then to combine them into a single scalar reward used by PPO. The combination is a piecewise rule governed by a single global safety-gating threshold. Writing p for a prompt and g for a sampled generation, the Llama 2 combined reward is:

    R_c(p, g) = R_s(p, g)   if  IS_SAFETY(p)  OR  R_s(p, g) < 0.15
              = R_h(p, g)   otherwise

after which the linear scores are whitened (the paper applies a logit transform and standardizes by the batch mean and standard deviation) so the combined signal balances against the PPO Kullback-Leibler (KL) penalty. Here IS_SAFETY(p) is a binary flag marking prompts drawn from the safety annotation set, and the constant 0.15 is the safety-gating threshold: any generation whose safety score falls below 0.15 is routed to the safety reward model (pushing the policy toward a safer response), while all other generations on non-flagged prompts are optimized purely for helpfulness. The number 0.15 is a single global scalar, fixed across the entire prompt distribution.

### 1.2 How the field combines multiple alignment objectives

The broader literature offers several families of methods for trading off or combining multiple alignment objectives, and it is useful to see where the Llama 2 gate sits among them.

- Single-reward RLHF and its successors. Classic RLHF [3,4,5] and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [10] optimize one preference signal. They do not separate safety from helpfulness at the reward-combination layer at all; the trade-off, if any, is baked into a single dataset.

- Constrained / safe RLHF. Safe RLHF (Dai et al. 2023 [17]) treats safety as an explicit constraint: it trains a separate cost (harm) model and solves a Lagrangian, adapting a dual variable (Lagrange multiplier) during training so that an expected-cost constraint is satisfied. This is the closest existing line to "two signals, arbitrated by a tunable quantity," but the arbitration variable is a single global dual variable updated over training, not a per-prompt function of an observable covariate.

- Multi-objective interpolation. Rewarded Soups [13], WARM [14], Multi-Objective DPO (MODPO) [15], and Directional Preference Alignment [16] combine objectives by interpolating model weights or reward vectors with a global mixing coefficient or preference direction. The coefficient is chosen globally (or exposed to the user as a global control), not conditioned per prompt on an observable risk signal.

- Reward-model robustness. Scaling-laws work on reward overoptimization [11] and reward-model ensembles [12,14] aggregate multiple reward models (mean, minimum, uncertainty penalty) to resist Goodharting. These methods aggregate over models, not over an observable per-prompt risk covariate, and they are not about the safety-vs-helpfulness gate.

- Rule- and AI-feedback-based safety. Rule-Based Rewards (Mu et al. 2024 [20]), Constitutional AI [8], and RLAIF [9] encode safety as global rules or AI-graded rubrics applied across the prompt distribution. The rules are global; they are not a per-prompt-covariate-conditional arbitration threshold with a guaranteed reduction to the Llama 2 gate.

- Adaptive regularization strength. beta-DPO [28] adapts the regularization strength beta of the preference objective at the batch level based on the observed reward gap. It adapts how hard to push, not the safety-vs-helpfulness arbitration threshold, and it conditions on a batch statistic rather than a per-prompt risk covariate.

### 1.3 How the field measures safety and over-refusal

Safety is evaluated with red-teaming and refusal benchmarks: HarmBench [24], Do-Not-Answer [23], ToxiGen [25], and judge models such as Llama Guard [21]. Crucially, the field has also recognized the dual failure mode -- exaggerated safety, or over-refusal of benign prompts -- and built XSTest [22] specifically to measure it: a suite of safe prompts that superficially resemble unsafe ones (for example, "how do I kill a Python process"), on which over-tuned models wrongly refuse. Helpfulness is measured with AlpacaFarm/AlpacaEval [26] and MT-Bench [27]. The existence of XSTest [22] is direct evidence that a single conservative safety policy systematically harms a benign tail -- exactly the tail that a global gating threshold cannot protect.

### 1.4 Datasets with joint helpfulness and safety labels

Reproducing the two-reward-model recipe outside Meta is now feasible because public preference datasets carry both a helpfulness preference and a harmlessness/safety label with harm-category tags: Anthropic HH-RLHF [7], BeaverTails [18], and PKU-SafeRLHF [19]. These supply the material to train R_h and R_s separately and to define a per-prompt risk covariate and harm-category bins.

### 1.5 The methodological gap this proposal targets

Across all of these lines, the Llama 2 safety-gating threshold 0.15 is treated as a single global engineering constant. No line treats the threshold as a per-prompt field conditioned on an observable risk covariate, and no line offers a way to relax the threshold on demonstrably benign prompts while provably leaving the original Llama 2 reward unchanged at a default setting. That is the gap this proposal formalizes and fills.


## Section 2. Key Findings and Analysis

### 2.1 The representational impossibility (GAP = MONOGATE)

We name the gap MONOGATE: the Monolithic safety-Gating threshold. The Llama 2 combined reward applies one global threshold tau = 0.15 identically to every prompt p, blind to the observable per-prompt risk d_p (how likely p is to elicit unsafe content). The optimal arbitration point is not constant: there is a per-prompt optimum tau*(p) that should be high (conservative) for genuinely risky prompts and low (helpfulness-favoring) for benign prompts. A single scalar tau cannot equal a non-constant tau*(p). Therefore one global threshold is necessarily wrong in two opposite directions at once:

- Too lax on high-risk prompts. On a genuinely risky prompt, a borderline-unsafe generation whose safety score sits just above 0.15 escapes the safety reward model and is optimized purely for helpfulness -- the gate fails to engage where it matters most.

- Too strict on benign prompts. On a safe-but-superficially-sensitive prompt (the XSTest [22] regime), the noisy safety model occasionally scores a perfectly helpful generation below 0.15, handing it to the safety reward model and teaching the policy to refuse or hedge -- manufacturing over-refusal where none is warranted.

The harm has three signatures that together explain why it has persisted:

- SILENT under pooled metrics. Aggregate helpfulness win-rate and aggregate safety-violation-rate average over the whole prompt distribution. The under-gating on the risky tail and the over-gating on the benign tail are opposite-signed deviations from per-prompt optimal, so they partially cancel in the pooled means. A model can look Pareto-optimal in aggregate while being simultaneously under-safe on a risky minority and over-refusing on a benign minority.

- SCALES with risk heterogeneity. The size of the loss grows with the spread (variance) of per-prompt risk d_p across the prompt distribution. The more a deployment mixes clearly-benign and clearly-risky prompts, the more a single tau is wrong for both ends.

- VANISHES under homogeneity. If every prompt has the same risk, one global threshold is optimal and there is nothing to gain. The gap is purely a heterogeneity phenomenon -- which is also what makes it falsifiable.

### 2.2 The fix (METHOD = AEGIS) and why it is the right shape

We name the method AEGIS: Adaptive, Empirical-bayes Gating by Inferred Sensitivity. AEGIS promotes the scalar tau to a per-prompt, risk-conditional threshold:

    tau(d_p) = clamp_[tau_min, tau_max]( tau_global + g_theta(d_p) )

with the following structure:

- Observable covariate d_p. d_p is a leakage-free per-prompt risk score computed from the safety reward model R_s that Llama 2 already trains, applied to K reference generations sampled from the FROZEN reference policy pi_ref. A concrete default: d_p = 1 - (1/K) sum_k sigmoid(R_s(p, g_k_ref)), the expected unsafety of prompt p under the frozen reference. Because d_p is computed from the frozen reference and the existing safety model -- never from the policy being updated and never from held-out evaluation labels -- it is observable at training time and leakage-free. Optionally d_p is fused with a harm-category prior from the dataset tags [18,19] via partial pooling.

- Structured monotone map. g_theta is monotone non-decreasing in risk, parameterized as a sum of monotone basis functions with NO constant term, so that theta = 0 makes g_theta(d) = 0 for ALL d (not merely at one point). The clamp keeps tau in a safe band; the clamp is inactive when tau_global lies inside [tau_min, tau_max]. Monotone direction: higher risk d_p raises tau, routing more borderline generations to the safety model (more conservative); lower risk lowers tau, letting the helpfulness model optimize freely (less over-refusal).

- Hierarchical partial pooling. Thresholds are estimated with empirical-Bayes partial pooling across K risk bins [30,31]: data-poor bins shrink toward the global default tau_global, and the deployable default is a single shared monotone map rather than free per-prompt parameters.

AEGIS changes only the reward-combination/gating layer of RLHF. It does not retrain either reward model, does not alter the policy architecture, the PPO algorithm, the KL penalty, or the SFT/preference data, and -- when d_p reuses R_s -- adds no new model. Computing d_p is a one-time training-time pre-pass over the frozen reference; it adds zero inference cost.

### 2.3 The exact-collapse identity (the safety-critical guarantee)

The keystone property is that AEGIS contains Llama 2-Chat exactly. Set theta = 0 (so g_theta is identically zero) and tau_global = 0.15 inside the clamp band. Then tau(d_p) = 0.15 for every prompt, so

    R_c_AEGIS(p, g) = R_c_Llama2(p, g)   for every (p, g),

and since whitening is a fixed monotone post-transform applied to identical pre-whiten scores with identical batch statistics, the whitened RL signal is identical as well. The PPO trajectory is therefore identical update-for-update. We gate this in continuous integration:

    E_collapse = max_over_(p,g) | R_c_AEGIS(p, g) - R_c_Llama2(p, g) |  <=  1e-6.

Because tau enters through a discontinuous gate, the relevant notion of "exact collapse" is reward-value identity for every (p, g) -- the quantity that drives the RL update -- which is what E_collapse measures. The collapse is nested: tau_global = 0.15 recovers Llama 2-Chat exactly, and sweeping tau_global recovers any global-threshold variant. This identity is what makes AEGIS safe to propose: at its default it is, bit for bit, the published recipe, so it can be shadow-deployed and can only ever be turned on where validation shows it does not increase harm.

### 2.4 The novelty wedge C1-C4

AEGIS is defined by four properties; the claim is that no prior line satisfies more than two of them simultaneously.

- C1 (layer and cost): operates only at the reward-combination/gating layer, reusing Llama 2's two existing reward models and frozen reference, training no new model, changing no policy/PPO/KL/data, and adding zero inference cost.
- C2 (observable covariate): conditions the threshold on an observable, leakage-free per-prompt risk covariate d_p.
- C3 (structured map): uses a structured monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled map of d_p -- neither a single global scalar nor an unpooled free per-prompt threshold.
- C4 (exact collapse): provably reduces to Llama 2's global-threshold combined reward, with E_collapse <= 1e-6.

No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4. The six near-miss families and the specific clauses each misses are detailed in Section 3 under Question 4.

### 2.5 Why the binding obstacle is metric blindness

The single most important analytical finding is that the gap is undetectable under the field's standard evaluation. Pooled helpfulness win-rate and pooled safety-violation-rate (the metrics used to validate alignment recipes) average away the per-prompt over- and under-gating because those errors are opposite-signed and partially cancel. Consequently the gap can only be seen with risk-stratified evaluation -- especially on the benign-but-superficially-risky tail measured by XSTest [22] -- together with covariate-scramble and homogeneous-null falsifiers. This is why the problem has hidden in plain sight inside a widely-read, widely-reproduced recipe.


## Section 3. Novel Research Idea (the 5q proposal)

**Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field, Machine Learning.** In Llama 2-style RLHF, the safety and helpfulness reward models are combined through a single global safety-gating threshold (tau = 0.15) applied identically to every prompt. Because the optimal arbitration point varies with per-prompt risk, one scalar is simultaneously too lax on risky prompts and too strict on benign ones; the resulting harm cancels under pooled metrics, scales with risk heterogeneity, and vanishes under homogeneity. Promoting the scalar to a monotone, partially-pooled function of an observable per-prompt risk covariate -- with a provable exact reduction to the original recipe at its default -- can recover helpfulness on benign prompts without conceding safety on risky ones.

**Here are the five core questions:**

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can the single global safety-gating threshold that Llama 2-Chat uses to arbitrate between its helpfulness and safety reward models during RLHF be replaced by a per-prompt, risk-conditional threshold -- a monotone, partially-pooled map of an observable per-prompt risk covariate -- that improves the helpfulness-safety trade-off on heterogeneous-risk prompts while provably collapsing to the original global-threshold reward at its default setting?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

This question matters because reward-model combination is the exact place where modern aligned assistants decide how to trade safety against helpfulness, and the Llama 2 recipe [1] -- including its 0.15 safety gate -- is one of the most widely read and reproduced alignment blueprints in the field. Five implications follow.

P1. It reframes a buried engineering constant as a learnable, accountable design surface. The 0.15 threshold is presented as a fixed number; this work shows it is a degenerate (constant) special case of a per-prompt function and gives a principled, auditable way to choose it. That reframing is reusable by anyone who combines a safety signal with a helpfulness signal, which is now the default for production assistants.

P2. It directly attacks the over-refusal problem. Exaggerated safety -- refusing benign prompts -- is a first-order usability and trust failure documented by XSTest [22] and widely felt by users. By lowering the gate only on prompts that an observable risk covariate certifies as benign, AEGIS offers a mechanism to recover helpfulness precisely where current models over-refuse, without a blanket loosening that would raise harm.

P3. It is deployable by construction, not just in principle. Because of the exact-collapse identity (Section 2.3), the method is a strict superset of the current recipe: its default is the published behavior, and any active configuration can be validation-gated and shadow-deployed. This lowers the barrier to adoption to near zero and makes the contribution immediately useful to practitioners rather than purely conceptual.

P4. It exposes a measurement blind spot the community should care about. The finding that pooled helpfulness and safety metrics average away per-prompt over- and under-gating implies that other "well-tuned" recipes may carry the same hidden tail damage. The risk-stratified evaluation protocol introduced here (per-risk-decile safety and over-refusal, plus the safe-helpfulness frontier) is a general diagnostic that future alignment papers can adopt.

P5. It generalizes a recurring pattern. Replacing a single global scalar with a monotone, partially-pooled map of an observable covariate, with an exact-collapse guarantee, is a template that has paid off across machine-learning subfields. Instantiating it on the safety-helpfulness gate connects LLM alignment to that broader methodology and invites cross-pollination (for example, with worst-group/distributionally-robust evaluation [29] and empirical-Bayes shrinkage [30,31]).

A paper answering this question would change how practitioners think about reward-model combination (from "pick a constant" to "fit a safe, observable, collapsible map"), give the community a concrete tool for reducing over-refusal without trading away safety, and supply an evaluation protocol that makes the hidden tail damage visible.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The difficulty is not implementing a prompt-dependent threshold; it is doing so in a way that provably helps the tails, provably does no harm at default, and is even detectable at all. Six obstacles, with O6 binding.

O1. Entangled per-prompt optimum. The per-prompt optimal threshold tau*(p) is confounded with prompt difficulty, the base policy's helpfulness on p, and reward-model noise. Naively regressing observed reward against tau per prompt recovers these confounds, not the risk-driven signal; the covariate effect must be isolated (residualized) from difficulty and base competence.

O2. Data poverty per prompt. RLHF sees few generations per prompt, so a free per-prompt threshold is wildly overparameterized and overfits sampling noise (this is the unpooled baseline B6 below, which we expect to fail). Some structure -- monotonicity plus partial pooling toward the global default -- is mandatory, not optional.

O3. Covariate observability and leakage-freeness. The covariate d_p must be computable at training time without peeking at the policy being updated (which would create a feedback loop) and without using held-out evaluation labels (which would leak). Building d_p from the frozen reference policy and the already-trained safety model satisfies this, but it must be argued carefully, because a covariate that drifts with the policy would silently break both the gradient and the collapse guarantee.

O4. Multi-axis risk. "Risk" is not one-dimensional: violence, self-harm, illicit instructions, privacy, and hate each have different acceptable operating points. A scalar d_p is a proxy; collapsing all harm types onto one axis can be wrong, so the method must support harm-category-aware bins while keeping the partially-pooled structure tractable.

O5. The triple constraint. The method must simultaneously (a) lift helpfulness on the benign tail, (b) not regress safety on the risky tail, and (c) reduce exactly to the base recipe at default. Naive prompt-dependent thresholds achieve at most one: a hand-tuned per-category threshold can help a tail but offers no exact collapse and no guarantee on the other tail; a learned free threshold can fit training reward but breaks both collapse and safety. Satisfying all three at once forces the specific construction (monotone, no-constant-term map; clamp; partial pooling; reuse of the existing safety model) used here.

O6 (BINDING). Metric blindness. Even a correct fix is invisible under the standard pooled helpfulness and safety metrics, because the per-prompt over- and under-gating cancel in the means (Section 2.5). Without risk-stratified evaluation and the right falsifiers, a true improvement on both tails can show up as a statistical wash, and -- worse -- a method that merely shifts harm from one tail to another can look like an improvement. Overcoming O6 requires building the stratified, frontier-based evaluation and the covariate-scramble / homogeneous-null / monotone-dose falsifiers BEFORE any gain can be credibly claimed. This is the obstacle that has kept the gap unsolved, and addressing it is a core contribution, not an afterthought.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Prior work has circled this exact mechanism without landing on it, for identifiable reasons. We position AEGIS against six near-miss families and show each misses at least two of the wedge clauses C1-C4 (Section 2.4).

NM1 -- Llama 2-Chat's global-threshold combined reward [1] (the collapse target). It uses a single global tau = 0.15. It satisfies C1 (it is the gating layer) but misses C2 (no covariate), C3 (a scalar, not a map), and C4 (nothing to collapse to). Misses three.

NM2 -- Safe RLHF / constrained RLHF (Dai et al. 2023 [17]) (the sharpest near-miss). It introduces a second (cost) model and arbitrates via a Lagrangian dual variable adapted during training. It is the closest prior art to "two signals arbitrated by a tunable quantity," and it arguably touches C1. But the dual variable is a single global scalar driven by an aggregate constraint, not a per-prompt function of an observable covariate (misses C2 and C3), and it provides no exact reduction to the Llama 2 gate (misses C4). Misses three.

NM3 -- Multi-objective interpolation: Rewarded Soups [13], WARM [14], MODPO [15], Directional Preference Alignment [16]. They combine objectives with a global mixing weight or preference direction. The mixing is global, not conditioned per prompt on observable risk (misses C2 and C3), and there is no collapse to the threshold gate (misses C4). Misses three (and most also miss C1, operating at the weight/representation layer rather than the gate).

NM4 -- Reward-model robustness: overoptimization scaling laws [11] and reward ensembles [12,14]. They aggregate over reward models (mean/min/uncertainty) to resist Goodharting. This is aggregation over models, not a per-prompt safety-gating threshold conditioned on observable risk (misses C2 and C3), with no collapse to the gate (misses C4). Misses three.

NM5 -- Rule- and AI-feedback safety: Rule-Based Rewards [20], Constitutional AI [8], RLAIF [9]. They encode safety as global rules or AI-graded rubrics applied across prompts. The rules are global (misses C2 and C3) and there is no exact reduction to the Llama 2 gate (misses C4); RBR also adds a graded reward component (touching C1). Misses three.

NM6 -- Adaptive regularization strength: beta-DPO [28]. It adapts the regularization strength beta at the batch level from the reward gap. It adapts how hard to push, not the safety-vs-helpfulness arbitration threshold, conditions on a batch statistic rather than a per-prompt covariate (misses C2 and C3), and does not collapse to the gate (misses C4). Misses three.

The barriers that kept the gap open. First, the 0.15 threshold was framed as a one-off engineering constant for arbitrating between two models, never as a per-prompt field; the safety model's own per-prompt score on reference samples -- a cheap, already-available signal -- was never repurposed as the threshold-selection covariate. Second, the field validates alignment with pooled helpfulness and safety metrics, which (Section 2.5) mathematically hide the per-prompt cancellation, so there was no pressure to look. Third, the triple constraint (benign-tail lift + risky-tail no-regression + exact collapse) was never co-targeted, so prior prompt-dependent ideas were rejected as either unsafe or not reducible to the trusted baseline. AEGIS differs by conditioning on an observable, leakage-free covariate (C2), using a monotone partially-pooled map rather than a scalar or free per-prompt value (C3), staying entirely at the gating layer with no new model (C1), and -- decisively -- carrying a build-time exact-collapse guarantee to the published recipe (C4).

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Method. Replace the global gate with tau(d_p) = clamp_[tau_min, tau_max](tau_global + g_theta(d_p)), where g_theta is monotone non-decreasing with no constant term (theta = 0 gives g identically zero), tau_global defaults to 0.15, and d_p = 1 - (1/K) sum_k sigmoid(R_s(p, g_k_ref)) is computed from the existing safety model R_s on K reference generations from the frozen reference policy (optionally fused with harm-category priors from dataset tags). Thresholds are fit by empirical-Bayes partial pooling across K risk bins, with data-poor bins shrinking to tau_global; the deployable default is one shared monotone map. The IS_SAFETY(p) OR-clause is preserved unchanged, so d_p is a continuous generalization of the binary safety flag, and AEGIS only varies the numeric threshold. The combined reward is otherwise computed and whitened exactly as in [1], and optimized with the same PPO setup [6].

Datasets. (i) Training/alignment: Anthropic HH-RLHF [7], BeaverTails [18], and PKU-SafeRLHF [19], each providing helpfulness preferences and harmlessness/safety labels with harm-category tags, used to train R_h and R_s and to define d_p and category bins. (ii) Base models: open Llama-2-7B and Llama-2-13B [1], SFT then two-reward-model RLHF, reproducing the Llama 2 combined-reward recipe as the primary baseline. (iii) Evaluation: risky tail -- BeaverTails-eval [18], HarmBench [24], Do-Not-Answer [23], ToxiGen [25], judged by Llama Guard [21] plus human spot-check; benign-but-flagged tail -- XSTest [22] safe subset (the key over-refusal probe); general helpfulness -- AlpacaFarm/AlpacaEval [26] and MT-Bench [27]; plus semi-synthetic prompt mixtures with controlled per-prompt risk spread for the dose-response test.

Baselines. B1 SFT-only; B2 helpfulness-RM-only RLHF (helpfulness ceiling, unsafe); B3 safety-RM-only RLHF (over-refusing); B4 Llama 2 global gate tau = 0.15 (PRIMARY collapse target and exact-identity guard); B5 best global tau, swept on a grid (the RETUNED single-global baseline used for the homogeneous-null falsifier); B6 unpooled free per-prompt threshold (overfit control); B7 covariate-scrambled threshold (placebo: d_p permuted across prompts); B8 per-prompt oracle threshold (grid-searched per prompt; impractical upper bound); B9 Safe RLHF Lagrangian [17] (sharpest competing method); B10 AEGIS.

Metrics. PRIMARY: the joint movement of (a) top-risk-decile safety-violation rate, which must NOT regress versus B4, and (b) benign-flagged-tail over-refusal rate (XSTest safe), which should drop versus B4; reported as a safe-helpfulness Pareto frontier (helpfulness at matched safety and safety at matched helpfulness, traced by threshold sweeps), with the claim being Pareto improvement over B4 and B5. SECONDARY: risk-stratified helpfulness-vs-risk and safety-vs-risk slopes; pooled helpfulness win-rate and pooled safety no-regression within a pre-registered margin; KL-to-reference matched across methods; risk-calibration of d_p (AUROC of d_p against ground-truth harm-elicitation labels); and the collapse guard E_collapse <= 1e-6.

Ablations. A1 covariate choice (R_s-on-reference vs harm-category prior vs fused); A2 monotone basis family; A3 number of risk bins K; A4 partial-pooling strength; A5 clamp band [tau_min, tau_max]; A6 with/without the IS_SAFETY-flag interaction; A7 robustness to safety-model label noise injected into R_s.

Falsifiers (the method must survive all three). T1 homogeneous-null: on a homogeneous-risk prompt set, AEGIS must NOT beat the RETUNED best-global-tau baseline B5 -- this separates a genuine heterogeneity gain from merely finding a better global constant, and is distinct from the theta = 0 exact-identity guard. T2 monotone-dose: as the per-prompt risk spread of the prompt distribution increases (controlled in the semi-synthetic mixtures), AEGIS's gain over B4 and B5 must increase monotonically. T3 covariate-scramble: scrambling d_p (baseline B7) must destroy the gain, confirming the gain comes from the covariate and not from extra capacity.

Expected outcomes (hypotheses; numeric ranges are [conjectured, no preliminary results]).
H1: AEGIS reduces benign-flagged over-refusal (XSTest safe) by [conjectured 15-40% relative] versus B4 with no increase in top-risk-decile harm rate.
H2: AEGIS Pareto-dominates B4 and B5 on the safe-helpfulness frontier, with the gain concentrated in high-risk-spread regimes.
H3: the gain scales monotonically with per-prompt risk spread (passes T2).
H4: covariate-scramble B7 erases at least [conjectured 80%] of the gain (passes T3).
H5: on a homogeneous-risk set, AEGIS is statistically indistinguishable from B5 (passes T1), confirming the mechanism is heterogeneity, not a better constant.
H6: E_collapse <= 1e-6 at theta = 0, with exact recovery of Llama 2-Chat -- verifiable by construction.

Responsible AI and Data-Governance Considerations. F1 (safety-first, do-no-harm): AEGIS must never increase harm on any risk bin; the global tau is the do-no-harm default, active per-prompt configurations are validation-gated behind a hard safety guardrail, and over-refusal reduction is pursued only where it provably does not raise harm. For the highest-severity categories (for example CBRN, self-harm, child-safety) tau is pinned to maximally conservative regardless of d_p (a hard safety floor), so the method only ever relaxes on demonstrably benign content. F2 (covariate fairness): d_p must not become a proxy for sensitive attributes, dialects, or languages (for example over-flagging African-American English or non-English text as risky); the covariate is content-risk, not author identity, and results are reported disaggregated by harm category and language/group, auditing the risk estimator for bias against protected groups. F3 (collapse/disable switch): the theta = 0 exact disable switch is shadow-deployable and always recoverable, with per-example logging of tau(d_p) and d_p for audit. F4 (scientific integrity): all numeric hypothesis ranges are marked [conjectured, no preliminary results]; the E_collapse unit test, the reproduction recipe, and the covariate estimator are released; and because the Llama Guard [21] judge is imperfect, harm claims are confirmed by human spot-check. F5 (governance/auditability): the threshold map tau(d_p), the covariate d_p, the risk estimator, the clamp band, and the bin definitions are governed and human-reviewed for high-stakes harm categories, and no active configuration ships until the safety guardrail clears it.


## Section 4. References and Evidence
